title: Nearly ballistic transport and high magnetic-field sensitivity in a $\text{Bi}_4\text{Br}_4$ topological Josephson weak link
abstract: Superconducting weak links with high transparency offer an appealing approach for designing compact magnetic-field sensors, as their phase-dependent Andreev bound-state (ABS) spectrum produces a strong flux-to-signal response with minimal dissipation. One way to achieve ballistic transport in the weak link is to use the edge states of topological insulators, since these states resist backscattering and provide a unique path to developing topological insulator-based weak-link devices for highly efficient magnetic-field sensing. Building on this, we propose a weak-link device using superconducting Nb electrodes and a nanoribbon of $\text{Bi}_4\text{Br}_4$ as the normal region, forming a Nb-1D (one-dimensional) $\text{Bi}_4\text{Br}_4$-Nb Josephson junction. We develop first-principles tight-binding Hamiltonians and orbital-resolved interface couplings in the Wannier basis, including spin-orbit coupling, based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The Eliashberg spectral function of bulk Nb, obtained via density functional perturbation theory (DFPT), indicates an electron-phonon coupling strength of 1.19 and a transition temperature of about 9 K, aligning well with conventional superconductivity in Nb. The subgap conductance is primarily influenced by Andreev processes. The ABS spectrum leads to a non-sinusoidal current-phase relation (CPR) with high forward skewness ($+$1.74) and phase sensitivity to the magnetic field. Overall, our findings suggest that the Nb-1D $\text{Bi}_4\text{Br}_4$-Nb weak link is a promising platform for on-chip superconducting magnetic sensors, compatible with scalable nanofabrication and broader development of topological-superconductor hybrid electronics.
summary: This work theoretically investigates using a topological insulator nanoribbon (Bi4Br4) as the weak link in a superconducting Josephson junction. By calculating the Andreev bound state spectrum and current-phase relation, the authors demonstrate that this structure achieves nearly ballistic transport. This results in a highly phase-sensitive supercurrent, positioning it as a promising platform for next-generation on-chip magnetic sensors.
main_problem: The goal is to design highly sensitive, compact magnetic-field sensors using superconducting weak links that exploit topological edge states for ballistic transport.
main_result: The Nb-1D Bi4Br4-Nb junction exhibits a non-sinusoidal current-phase relation with high forward skewness, indicating strong phase sensitivity suitable for advanced magnetic sensing applications.
method: Theoretical calculations employing Density Functional Theory (DFT) and the Non-Equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) formalism are used to model quasiparticle transport and Andreev bound states (ABS).
model_or_system: The system is a Josephson junction formed by superconducting Niobium (Nb) electrodes connected via a one-dimensional nanoribbon of the topological insulator Bi4Br4. The physics relies on proximity-induced superconductivity into the topologically protected edge states.
key_observables: Non-sinusoidal Current-Phase Relation (CPR), high forward skewness (+1.74), and phase sensitivity to external magnetic fields.
important_parameters: The ratio of junction length to coherence length ($\lambda = L/\xi$), the superconducting gap ($\Delta \approx 4$ meV for Nb), and the transmission coefficient ($	au$).
main_assumptions: The topological edge states are treated as normal metallic channels exhibiting ballistic transport, and the bulk superconductors (Nb) are modeled using conventional BCS-like s-wave pairing.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate ABS energy-phase dispersion curves showing thickness dependence and avoided crossings; other figures map out the CPR vs. phase ($\varphi$) at various magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the need for high-transparency weak links, detailing the theoretical framework (DFT/Wannier basis), calculating the ABS spectrum, deriving the Josephson current dependence on phase and thickness, and finally analyzing how external magnetic fields tune this phase sensitivity.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter superconductivity, the strong reliance on topological edge states and quantum confinement effects in a mesoscopic junction makes it relevant to open quantum systems modeling where boundary conditions dictate macroscopic behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 4. Calculated normal-state transmission and superconducting subgap conductance of Nb—1D Bi4Br4—Nb weak link.
Figure 5. Andreev bound states of Nb—1D Bi4Br4—Nb weak link, considering both single-channel and multichannel models.
